Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Mar-23-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. KASSRA NASSIRI, ESQ. of Nassiri & Jung LLP, Tel No. 415-762-3100 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. JOSEPH C. LIBURT, ESQ. of Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe LLP, Tel No. 650-614-7447 appearing for Defendant Bitfury Group Limited and Bitfury USA, Inc.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. As to DEFENDANTS BITFURY GROUP LIMITED AND BITFURY USA, INC.'S Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint, Defendants' motion to strike is overruled as to plaintiff's requests for specific performance, granted with twenty days leave to amend as to plaintiff's requests for an accounting and punitive damages sought for the fifth cause of action for wrongful discharge in violation of public policy, and granted without leave to amend as to plaintiff's request for punitive damages for the eighth cause of action for promissory estoppel. Plaintiff adequately pleads entitlement to specific performance in the event that money damages are unascertainable. Alleging that money damages are unascertainable is the functional equivalent to alleging that such damages are inadequate. As to his requests for an accounting, plaintiff insufficiently alleges that the accounts are so complicated that an ordinary legal action seeking a fixed sum is impracticable, but he is given an opportunity to make such allegations if he can do so in good faith. (Civic Western Corp. v. Zila Industries, Inc. (1977) 66 Cal.App.3d 1, 14.) Plaintiff is also given leave to allege facts that he was unlawfully discharged as a result of malicious, fraudulent or oppressive conduct by defendants. His request for punitive damages on his promissory estoppel claim fails as a matter of a law. (CalFarm Ins. Co. v. Krusiewicz (2005) 131 Cal.App.4th 273, 285-86.) Without objection from defendants, Plaintiff is also granted leave to amend the complaint re: Defendant Vavilov who has now been served and is now appearing in the case. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-23-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. KASSRA NASSIRI, ESQ. of Nassiri & Jung LLP, Tel No. 415-762-3100 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. JOSEPH C. LIBURT, ESQ. of Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe LLP, Tel No. 650-614-7447 appearing for Defendant Bitfury Group Limited and Bitfury USA, Inc.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant Bitfury USA's demurrer to the first, second, third, fifth, sixth, seventh, eighth and ninth causes of action on the grounds that plaintiff did not adequately plead an employer-employee relationship is overruled. Defendants Bitfury USA and Bitfury Group's demurrer is overruled as to the fifth, seventh, and ninth causes of action and sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the third cause of action for breach of the implied covenant, the eighth cause of action for promissory estoppel and the tenth cause of action for defamation. Plaintiff adequately pleads both alter ego liability against Bitfury USA and that Bitfury USA was his employer. Plaintiff also adequately alleges claims for wrongful termination in violation of public policy, violation of section 17200 and for restitution based on unjust enrichment. (Gould v. Maryland Sound Industries, Inc. (19095) 31 Cal. App. 4gh 1137, 1147; Shroyer v. New Cingular Wirless Services, Inc. (9th Cir. 2010) 622 F.3d 1035, 1044; McBride v. Boughton (2004) 123 Cal. App. 4th 379, 387). If he can do so in good faith, plaintiff is given leave to remedy the deficiency in his claim for breach of the implied covenant by alleging facts showing that his discharge was a pretext to deprive him of other contract benefits. (Guz v. Bechtel National, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 317.) If he can do so in good faith, plaintiff is granted leave to amend his eighth cause of action for promissory estoppel to assert a claim that is not premised on his providing consideration in exchange for the alleged promise. (Fontenot v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 256, 275.) Lastly, if he can do so in good faith, plaintiff is granted leave to amend his tenth cause of action for defamation to plead the specific defamatory statements allegedly made by defendants. (Kahn v. Bower (1991) 232 Cal.App.3d 1599, 1612, n. 5.) Without objection from defendants, Plaintiff is also granted leave to amend the complaint re: Defendant Vavilov who has now been served and is now appearing in the case. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)

Notice Of Motion To Quash Service
Off Calendar Jan-15-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: J. Raul Alcantar of Nassiri & Jung LLP telephone number 415-762-3100 for Plaintiff.
Joseph Liburt of Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe LLP telephone number 650-614-7447 for Defendant.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT VALERIJS VAVILOVS'S Motion To Quash Service is denied. Plaintiff has the burden of proving the facts required to establish the validity of service on the defendant. (Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, 413.) The filing of a proof of service of summons creates a rebuttable presumption that service was proper. (Dill v. Berquist Constr. Co. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 1426, 1447.) Mr. Simons, the process server, filed a proof of service of summons. When men are within easy speaking distance of each other and facts occur that would convince a reasonable man that personal service of a legal document is being attempted, service cannot be avoided by denying service and moving away without consenting to take the document in hand. (Khourie Crew & Jaeger v. Sabek, Inc. citing In re Ball (1934) 2 Cal.App.2d 578, 579.) Mr. Simons' declaration that on November 4, 2015, he called out Defendant's name in the lobby of the Four Season Hotel on 747 Market Street, San Francisco, he shook hands with Defendant, he informed Defendant that he was being served, he attempted to hand the documents to Defendant, and Defendant "refused to accept graciously and then dropped the papers," indicates that Defendant was personally served as defined in In re Bell. (Declaration of Scott Simons' Declaration paragraph 3.) Mr. Simons has not perjured himself in his declaration and Defendant's declaration does not overcome the rebuttable presumption that service was proper. The motion to quash service on the grounds that Plaintiff failed to comply with the service abroad requirements is rendered moot by the court's ruling that defendant was personally served on November 4, 2015. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Ernest Goldsmith. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/EG)
